local.change_request: Replace the JSON.stringify-based sha256 etag in src/snapshots.ts with the object-hash library so snapshot etags are stable regardless of property insertion order. Keep the existing SNAPSHOTS_DIR layout, the 64-char hex etag shape (i.e. configure object-hash to use sha256), and all current TreeSnapshotStore.put/get callers. Tests in index.test.ts (snapshot store: same tree -> same etag, different tree -> different etag) must continue to pass.
local.intent_analysis:
Type: refactor (replace internal hashing implementation; no behaviour change visible to users).

What is being asked for:
- Swap the etag function in src/snapshots.ts from `sha256(JSON.stringify(parsed))` to a call that uses the `object-hash` npm package.
- Object-hash sorts object keys recursively before hashing, eliminating the latent bug where two semantically identical ParsedTree objects can produce different etags depending on property insertion order in the in-memory representation.

Systems / files likely affected:
- src/snapshots.ts — the `computeEtag` function and `TreeSnapshotStore.put` (the only producer of etags). Swap the hash call.
- package.json + bun.lock — add `object-hash` as a runtime dependency. Add `@types/object-hash` if zero-dep typings aren't shipped.
- index.test.ts — three snapshot tests assert the etag matches /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/ (sha256 hex length). Configure object-hash with `algorithm: 'sha256', encoding: 'hex'` so the regex still matches and existing snapshot files are NOT silently invalidated for users who have on-disk state.
- src/commands.ts (4 callers) and src/mermaid.ts (1 caller) only consume the opaque etag; no edits needed.

Constraints:
- Etag shape stays the same (64-char lowercase hex) so the test suite + any downstream consumer's regex still works.
- The on-disk filename layout (`<SNAPSHOTS_DIR>/<etag>.json`) is unchanged.
- No backwards-compat shim needed — the change deliberately invalidates *prior* etags (that is the whole point: prior etags were unstable). New etags computed against existing trees may differ from any user's currently-cached value, but on first miss `TreeSnapshotStore.put` will write the new file and reads continue to succeed because `computeEtag` is the only producer.

Codeowners:
- No CODEOWNERS file in this repo. The project owner reviews all snapshot-store changes; the plan ships with reviewed_by empty for them to populate.

Acceptance criteria:
- `object-hash` (and its types if needed) appears in package.json dependencies; bun install completes cleanly.
- src/snapshots.ts no longer imports node:crypto for the etag path; computeEtag delegates to object-hash with sha256/hex.
- bun test passes; specifically the three snapshot store tests in index.test.ts continue to pass without regex/length changes.
- Reordering keys in a ParsedTree literal (e.g. swap `local` and `global` in the constructor) produces the same etag — covered by a new test case.
local.draft_plan:
---
id: 1778371337-stable-etag-via-object-hash
title: Stable Snapshot Etags via object-hash
status: draft
author: Jonathan Turnock
created: 2026-05-10
reviewed_by:
---

## Summary

Replace the JSON.stringify-based sha256 etag in `src/snapshots.ts` with `object-hash`, which sorts object keys recursively before hashing. This eliminates the latent bug where two semantically identical `ParsedTree` objects can produce different etags depending on property insertion order, and keeps the existing `<etag>.json` snapshot-store layout untouched.

## Requirements

- `computeEtag(parsed)` returns the same 64-char lowercase hex string for any two `ParsedTree` values that are deeply equal, regardless of key ordering.
- The on-disk snapshot layout (`<SNAPSHOTS_DIR>/<etag>.json`) and the public `TreeSnapshotStore.put` / `TreeSnapshotStore.get` shapes are unchanged.
- All existing snapshot-store tests in `index.test.ts` (matching `/^[0-9a-f]{64}$/`) continue to pass without regex or length changes.
- A new test asserts key-order invariance: building a `ParsedTree` with `{ local, global, root }` versus `{ root, global, local }` produces the same etag.

## Technical Approach

1. Add `object-hash` (and `@types/object-hash` if the package does not ship its own typings) as a runtime dependency in `package.json`. Run `bun install` to update `bun.lock`.
2. In `src/snapshots.ts`:
   - Drop the `import { createHash } from "node:crypto"`.
   - Import `objectHash` (or default-export equivalent) from `object-hash`.
   - Replace `computeEtag` body with `objectHash(parsed, { algorithm: "sha256", encoding: "hex" })`. Keep the function signature `(parsed: ParsedTree): string`.
3. No edits to call-sites — `TreeSnapshotStore.put`, `commands.ts` (4 sites), `mermaid.ts` (1 site) treat the etag as opaque.
4. Add one test case to `index.test.ts` that constructs two `ParsedTree`-shaped fixtures with reordered top-level keys and asserts `computeEtag(a) === computeEtag(b)`. Test imports `computeEtag` directly from `src/snapshots.ts` (so the assertion does not require spinning up the CLI).

## Affected Systems

- `src/snapshots.ts` — implementation swap.
- `package.json`, `bun.lock` — new dependency.
- `index.test.ts` — one new test case for key-order invariance; existing snapshot tests unchanged.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bun install` completes with no audit warnings on `object-hash`.
- `grep -n createHash src/snapshots.ts` returns no matches; `grep -n object-hash src/snapshots.ts` returns one import line.
- `bun test` passes; the existing three snapshot-store tests in `index.test.ts` are green.
- The new key-order-invariance test passes.
- Manually re-running an existing execution with stale on-disk snapshots: `abtree next <existing-id>` either hits the existing snapshot file (etag unchanged for that exact insertion order) or fails cleanly with the existing 'Missing snapshot' message — never silently corrupts state.

## Risks & Considerations

- **Etag values change for users with on-disk state.** Any user who has existing executions whose `snapshot` field references a now-invalid etag will see `Missing snapshot: <etag>` on the next tick. Mitigation: this is the existing failure mode for snapshot-file deletion, so the error path is already covered. Document the upgrade as a snapshot-cache invalidation.
- **Performance.** `object-hash` walks the object instead of `JSON.stringify`-ing it; for the trees in this repo (largest is improve-codebase at ~388 lines of YAML, well under 100 KB resolved) this is negligible. No need for benchmarking.
- **Supply chain.** `object-hash` is a small, widely-used package (~30M downloads/week, MIT, no transitive deps). Acceptable.
- **Algorithm choice.** Forcing `sha256` keeps the etag length at 64 hex chars to avoid breaking the test regex. Future-us could switch to a shorter hash later by relaxing the regex; out of scope here.

## Open Questions

- Should the new key-order-invariance test live in `index.test.ts` (matching where the other snapshot tests are) or in a dedicated `tests/snapshots.test.ts`? Default: `index.test.ts`, to keep the diff minimal.
- Bun bundles `object-hash` fine in compiled binaries (we publish via `bun build --compile` per `scripts/build.ts`); flag for codeowner only if there is a known caveat.
local.hardened_plan:
---
id: 1778371337-stable-etag-via-object-hash
title: Stable Snapshot Etags via object-hash
status: draft
author: Jonathan Turnock
created: 2026-05-10
reviewed_by:
---

## Summary

Replace the JSON.stringify-based sha256 etag in `src/snapshots.ts` with the `object-hash` library, which sorts object keys recursively before hashing. This eliminates the latent bug where two `ParsedTree` values that are deeply equal can produce different etags depending on the property insertion order of the in-memory representation. The on-disk snapshot store layout is unchanged; only the algorithm producing the filename changes.

## Requirements

- `computeEtag(parsed)` returns the same 64-char lowercase hex string for any two `ParsedTree` values that are deep-equal, where deep-equality means: the same scalar field values, the same `children` arrays in the same order, and the same set of object keys at every level — independent of the key insertion order in either object literal.
- The on-disk layout (`<SNAPSHOTS_DIR>/<etag>.json`) and the public `TreeSnapshotStore.put` / `TreeSnapshotStore.get` signatures are unchanged.
- The three existing snapshot-store tests in `index.test.ts` (etag matches `/^[0-9a-f]{64}$/`, two executions of the same tree share one snapshot file, deleting the snapshot file makes reads fail) all pass without edits to their assertions.
- A new test asserts key-order invariance: two `ParsedTree` literals with identical content but different top-level key ordering produce the same etag.

## Technical Approach

1. **Add the dependency.** Add `object-hash` to `dependencies` in `package.json`. The package ships its own typings (`index.d.ts`) since v3, so `@types/object-hash` is not needed. Pin a version (e.g. `^3.0.0`); `bun install` updates `bun.lock`.
2. **Swap the implementation in `src/snapshots.ts`.**
   - Remove `import { createHash } from "node:crypto"`.
   - Add `import objectHash from "object-hash"`.
   - Replace `computeEtag`'s body with `return objectHash(parsed, { algorithm: "sha256", encoding: "hex" })`. Function signature `(parsed: ParsedTree): string` is unchanged.
3. **No call-site edits.** `TreeSnapshotStore.put` / `.get` are unchanged. `commands.ts` (4 sites) and `mermaid.ts` (1 site) treat the etag as opaque.
4. **Add the invariance test.** Append one test to `index.test.ts` that imports `computeEtag` directly from `src/snapshots.ts`, builds two minimal `ParsedTree` objects with reordered top-level keys (`{ local, global, root }` vs `{ root, global, local }`), and asserts equal etags. No CLI subprocess required.

## Affected Systems

- `src/snapshots.ts` — `computeEtag` body and imports.
- `package.json` (and `bun.lock`, regenerated) — new runtime dependency.
- `index.test.ts` — one new test case for key-order invariance.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `bun install` exits 0 with `object-hash@^3` resolved in `bun.lock`.
- `grep -n "createHash" src/snapshots.ts` returns no matches; `grep -n "object-hash" src/snapshots.ts` returns one import line.
- `bun test` passes. The three pre-existing snapshot-store tests in `index.test.ts` are green without modification (etag-format regex, snapshot-sharing, missing-snapshot-error).
- The new key-order-invariance test passes.
- `bun run scripts/build.ts` (single-target) produces a working binary (smoke check that `object-hash` bundles into the compiled output).

## Risks & Considerations

- **Etag values change for any user with on-disk state.** Existing executions whose persisted `snapshot` field references a previous etag will fail on the next tick with the existing `Missing snapshot: <etag>` error. This is the same error path already covered by the snapshot-deletion test, so behaviour is well-defined; no silent corruption is possible. Action: mention the snapshot-cache invalidation in the release notes.
- **Performance.** `object-hash` walks the object rather than calling `JSON.stringify`. For the trees in this repo (largest fully-resolved snapshot is well under 100 KB) this is negligible and not worth benchmarking.
- **Supply chain.** `object-hash` is small, widely used, MIT-licensed, with no runtime dependencies.

## Open Questions

None.
